Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd usually wait until something was due and then cram for a few hours. With six online courses, I just bounced around between whatever needed attention most urgently. Sometimes I'd listen to lectures while doing other stuff around the apartment, but I wouldn't call it organized at all.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place depending on deadlines. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, other weeks I'd be up late trying to catch up on multiple courses at once. Being 27 and dealing with limited resources means I've got other things competing for my time, so studying happened whenever I could squeeze it in rather than on any set schedule.
Q3: Usually a deadline was what got me to log in. I'd get an email reminder that something was due in 24 hours and that would be my cue. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything urgent I was missing, but honestly, most of the time it was panic-driven rather than proactive.
Q4: Not really, no. I never got into a groove where logging in felt automatic. It always felt like a chore I was putting off. The only semi-routine thing was checking in with a few classmates I'd connected with, but even that wasn't consistent. The LMS never became something I just naturally did.
Q5: The audio lectures were the most useful for me since I learn better by listening. I could download them and play them back while commuting or doing chores. The discussion board was okay when other students actually explained things in plain language. Anything I could listen to rather than read worked better for my brain.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the extra reading materials and supplementary videos. They felt like busywork and I was already stretched thin. The discussion forums too—I only posted once the whole course because it felt pointless to type out responses nobody would really engage with. If it wasn't going to directly help me pass, I skipped it.
Q7: If something was going to show up on an exam or was required for an assignment, it felt worth my time. I also valued anything that explained concepts quickly without a lot of fluff. When I could tell something was just padding the course content, I tuned out immediately. I needed efficiency because I had too much on my plate.
Q8: Optional stuff felt completely skippable to me. If it wasn't graded, my brain just filed it under 'ignore.' Required activities at least had consequences, so I'd make myself do them even when bored. The optional materials might have been helpful, but I never had the energy or motivation to find out.
Q9: Yeah, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download the audio lectures and listen to them on my phone when I wasn't near my computer. I also used some YouTube videos to understand concepts the course materials didn't explain well. None of that would show up in the system, but it's how I actually learned most of the content.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was reviewing my notes or re-listening to downloaded lectures, but the LMS wouldn't know about any of that. I'd also study with a couple of classmates in person sometimes—we'd go over material together, quiz each other. That peer interaction was helpful but completely invisible to the system.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture once, maybe watch the first few minutes online, then finish it offline later. Same with PDFs—I'd save them to my phone and read them on the bus. The LMS probably thought I barely touched those materials when really I'd gone through them multiple times offline.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got harder. I was stressed about money, dealing with some personal stuff, and juggling all six courses at once. My attendance dropped because I just couldn't bring myself to care anymore. The boredom set in hard—nothing felt interesting or worth the effort. I was just going through the motions.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about bills, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. I'd log in, stare at the screen for a while, then close it without doing much. The fatigue made everything feel twice as hard, and competing responsibilities always won out over coursework.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd have a video playing but I'd be zoned out thinking about other stuff. Or I'd scroll through readings without actually absorbing anything. My eyes were on the screen but my brain was somewhere else entirely. It was like going through the motions without actually being present.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially discussion posts and non-urgent assignments. I knew they mattered for my grade, but I just couldn't make myself care enough in the moment. The boredom made everything feel like a slog. I'd tell myself I'd do it later, and later would become the night before it was due.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. Without them, I probably wouldn't have done anything. I'd watch the countdown and wait until the pressure was high enough to overcome my inertia. It wasn't healthy, but that urgency was the only reliable motivator I had.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change much for me. By the time I got comments back, I'd already moved on mentally. My assignment scores were actually pretty good when I submitted things, but that didn't motivate me to engage more with the course overall. It was more like 'okay, I passed that one, onto the next crisis.'
Q18: The structure felt pretty rigid and didn't really work for how I learn. Everything was laid out in a way that assumed you'd engage consistently throughout the week, but that's not how my life works. The peer support intervention helped a bit—connecting with other students gave me some accountability. But the overall structure didn't accommodate people like me who drift in and out.
Q19: The heavy reliance on reading made things harder for me since I'm more of an auditory learner. When there were good audio or video components, I engaged more. The discussion requirements felt forced and pointless. Having more flexibility in how we could demonstrate learning would have helped me stay engaged instead of just checking boxes.
Q20: I think those students might be like me—logging in, clicking around, maybe starting things but not finishing them. Or they're looking for information but getting overwhelmed and giving up before submitting. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes you're just wandering around the system trying to figure out what you're supposed to do.
Q21: That's basically me. My LMS activity was low, but I passed and my assignment scores were actually really high when I submitted. I did a lot of learning offline—listening to lectures, studying with peers, using external resources. The system only sees part of the picture. Some of us just don't learn well through constant platform engagement.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you were actually paying attention or learning anything. They miss all the offline studying, the peer conversations, the thinking you do while commuting. They measure presence, not engagement. They count clicks, not comprehension.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling like it matters. It's not just showing up—it's being mentally present and wanting to understand. For me, engagement was rare this semester. I was mostly just surviving, doing the minimum to pass. Real engagement would mean I actually looked forward to learning the material.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on peer support to get through the course, or how my financial stress affected my ability to focus. It wouldn't capture the learning I did offline or the conversations with classmates that helped concepts click. It also wouldn't show that despite being bored and disengaged on the surface, I actually did absorb the material well enough to score high on assignments when I finally sat down to do them.
